- Wear goggles and gloves when attaching or removing gauges to
transfer refrigerant or to check pressures.
- Wear warm clothing when working in a walk-in cooler or freezer. A
technician does not think properly when cold.
- Be careful not to get your hands or clothing caught in moving parts
such as pulleys, belts, or fan blades.
- Wear an approved back brace or belt when lifting and use your legs,
keeping your back straight and upright.
- Observe all electrical safety precautions. Be careful at all times and use
common sense.
- Never work near live electrical circuits unless absolutely necessary.
Turn the power off at the nearest disconnect, lock and tag the panel,
and keep the only key in your possession.

#### 25.1 Introduction To Special
#### Refrigeration System
#### COMPONENTS
The basic vapor-compression refrigeration cycle, and the
four main components that make it up, has been discussed
throughout this text. These four components, namely the
compressor, the condenser, the evaporator, and the expansion
device allow the cycle to continually repeat itself as long as it
is operating, without depleting or otherwise affecting the system refrigerant. However, relying solely on these four system
components does not ensure that the system will operate as
effectively or efficiently as it can. To enhance system operation, reliability, and performance, additional devices and
components can be added to the refrigeration system. These
additional devices and components are commonly referred to
as special refrigeration system components and can be categorized in a number of different ways. The two broadest
categories for special HVACR system components are controls and accessories.
Controls can be classified as either operational or safety
devices. Operational controls open and close during normal
system operation, while safety controls are intended to stop
system operation in the event unsafe conditions are present.
Control devices can be mechanical, electrical, electromechanical, or electronic devices. Mechanical controls are generally
used to stop, start, or modulate fluid flow and can be operated by pressure, temperature, or other means. These controls
can usually be identified by their location, because they are
almost always found in the piping. Accessories are intended
to enhance or improve overall system performance. Because
there are so many different classifications of HVACR special system components and components themselves, some
devices may fall in more than one category. It is important
to realize, therefore, that because one particular system component is discussed in one section of this unit, it is not necessarily excluded from other sections.

605


---

## PDF Page 204

606

Section 5 Commercial Refrigeration

#### Evaporator Pressure Regulator
The evaporator pressure-regulating (EPR) valve, Figure!25.1,
is a mechanical control that keeps the refrigerant pressure
in the evaporator from going below a predetermined point.
Two pressures, the evaporator outlet pressure, which is the
valve’s inlet pressure, and the valve’s spring pressure, control the positioning of the valve. The evaporator pressure
and spring pressure counteract one another. The spring
pressure is a closing force, and the evaporator pressure is
an opening force. The outlet pressure is effectively cancelled
out and does not affect the valve operation because the area
of the bellows is equal to the area of the inlet port. The outlet pressure acts on these equal areas, but in opposite directions, thus they cancel one another, Figure 25.2. EPR valves
are also referred to as open on rise of inlet pressure (ORI)
valves, because they will throttle open on a rise in their
inlet (evaporator) pressure. When the evaporator is under a
high load, the EPR valve will be fully opened—in effect, act
as if it were not there. The spring pressure can be adjusted
with a screwdriver or Allen wrench to allow the setting of
a minimum evaporator pressure. Remember, these valves
simply establish a minimum evaporator pressure; they do

Figure 25.1 The EPR valve modulates the flow of vapor refrigerant
leaving the evaporator. It limits the pressure in the evaporator and keeps
it from dropping below the set point. Courtesy Sporlan Division, Parker
Hannifin Corporation

#### Adjustment For
#### Spring Pressure

#### To Compressor

#### Pressure Tap

#### FROM
#### EVAPORATOR

Figure 25.2 The cutaway view of a direct-acting evaporator pressureregulating (EPR) valve. Courtesy Sporlan Division, Parker Hannifin
Corporation

#### Adjusting Spring
#### BELLOWS

#### SEAT
#### FROM
#### EVAPORATOR

#### 25.2 Evaporator Controls

#### SEAT
#### DISC

#### To Compressor

#### Access Valve

not hold a constant pressure or limit maximum pressure in
the evaporator.
The EPR valve is installed in the suction line at the outlet of the evaporator. The bellows in the EPR valve senses
evaporator pressure and throttles (modulates) the suction
gas to the compressor. This will then allow the evaporator pressure to go as low as the pressure setting on the
valve. When the EPR valve is used with the thermostatic
expansion valve (TXV), the system can maintain a constant
superheat and keep the pressure from going too low. If the
pressure in the evaporator is lower than the EPR valve’s set
point, the EPR valve will close, causing refrigerant to back
up into the evaporator coil until the minimum pressure
level in the evaporator has been reached. As the evaporator
pressure rises above the minimum pressure setting, the EPR
valve will throttle open to allow excess pressure to flow
from the evaporator coil into the suction line and on to
the!compressor.
The EPR valve can be used in a system that cools water
to keep the evaporator from going below a predetermined
point, which could be freezing. For example, if there is a
load on the system at start-up, the EPR valve will be wide
open, and the TXV will throttle the flow of liquid refrigerant into the evaporator. When the evaporator pressure
reaches the point at which the EPR valve is set, the valve
will begin to throttle to slow the flow of refrigerant. If the
setting is just above freezing, the valve will throttle just
enough to keep the evaporator from freezing until the thermostat responds and shuts the system off, Figure 25.3. The
EPR valve is often called a “holdback valve” in the industry
because it holds the refrigerant back in the evaporator to
prevent the suction pressure in the evaporator from going
below a set point.

after the EPR valve starts to throttle.

#### COMPRESSOR

The EPR valve may operate for an extended period of
time while in the throttled-down position in order to satisfy the cooling load and keep the pressure in the evaporator from falling below a minimum set point. Many times,
the evaporator pressure will be close to the EPR’s set point
pressure, but some refrigeration is still needed due to a
small heat load on the evaporator. The flow of refrigerant
will be minimal, and the TXV may not be able to keep
a constant evaporator superheat because of the reduced
mass flow rate of refrigerant. High evaporator superheat
readings are often encountered at these throttled-down
conditions. However, once the heat load on the evaporator
increases and the EPR valve opens more to increase the
mass flow rate of refrigerant, the TXV will regain control
of evaporator superheat.
Larger Btu capacities, as in supermarket installations, call
for larger EPR valves or pilot-operated valves, Figure 25.4.
Some pilot-operated EPR valves are equipped with additional features such as a suction stop and an automatic close
feature, Figure 25.5. Some normally open pilot EPR valves
operate by high-pressure gas. These valves are held open
by a spring and are forced closed by high-side gas pressure,
Figure 25.6. Three pressures control the modulation of the
main valve piston: the high-pressure source, the evaporator
pressure, and a spring pressure. A pilot port that is controlled
by a pilot regulator valve indirectly controls the operation
of the main regulating valve. These valves include a solenoid
stop feature when hot gas defrost is used because the hot gas

Figure 25.6 An internal cutaway view of a pilot-operated EPR valve
showing springs, high-pressure source connection, pilot regulator, main
piston, and solenoid stop feature. Courtesy Sporlan Division, Parker Hannifin Corporation

#### PRESSURE
#### SOURCE
#### CONNECTION

is injected into the suction line between the EPR valve and
the coils being defrosted. A solenoid valve in the EPR valve
will be deenergized, shutting off the suction inlet pressure.
This allows the internal pilot spring to force the EPR valve

#### Suction Line

#### Multiple Evaporator Systems
Some refrigeration systems operate with a single compressor
that is used in conjunction with multiple evaporators. These
evaporators, depending on the system, might be located in
separate refrigerated boxes and might operate at different
temperatures and pressures. When such is the case, EPR
valves are used to allow each evaporator to operate at its
own, unique pressure and temperature. Consider the example
when one R-134a compressor is used with two evaporators,
one operating at a pressure of 18.4 psig (20°F for R-134a)
and the other operating at a pressure of 26.1 psig (30°F for
R-134a). To allow these two evaporators, that share a suction line, to operate at different pressures, an EPR valve is
needed at the outlet of the higher-temperature evaporator,
Figure 25.8. Please note that Figure 25.8 is showing only
the low pressure side of the refrigeration system. The true
suction pressure for the system will be the lower value of
18.4!psig, but the higher-temperature evaporator will operate
at a pressure of 26.1 psig. Several evaporators with different pressure requirements can be piped together using this
method, Figure!25.9. Notice that, in Figure 25.9, EPR valves
are installed at the outlet of all evaporators, with the exception of the lowest temperature evaporator which, in this
case, is operating at a pressure of 15 psig. Please note that
Figure!25.9 is showing only the low pressure side of the refrigeration system.

#### COMPRESSOR

It is desirable to know the actual pressure in the evaporator because that is not the same as the true suction pressure. Normally, a gauge port, known as a Schrader valve
port, is permanently installed in the EPR valve body, which
allows the service technician to take gauge readings on the
evaporator side of the valve, Figure 25.1. The common
suction-line pressure can be obtained at the compressor
service valve.

#### Electric Evaporator Pressureregulating (Eepr) Valve
As a result of regulatory changes in the U.S. Food and Drug
Administration’s (FDA) Food Code concerning product temperature in supermarkets, more sophisticated and accurate ways to
control the discharge air temperatures in the evaporator section
of display cases were put in place. Electric evaporator pressureregulating (EEPR) valves, Figure 25.10, designed for close temperature control, move in very small increments in response
to the sensed temperature of the evaporator’s discharge air.


---

609

As with traditional EPR valves, EEPR valves are located
at the evaporator outlet for single evaporator applications.
In multiple evaporator applications, they are located on the
suction line before the common suction header as in parallel
compressor systems, Figure 25.11. EEPR valves and a refrigerated case discharge air sensor are wired to a microprocessor board for control purposes.
Although EEPR valves are referred to as evaporator
pressure regulators, they do not serve the same purpose as
the standard EPR valve mentioned earlier. A standard mechanical EPR valve prevents pressure, and thus temperature,
from falling below a minimum in the evaporator. EEPR
valves, on the other hand, control the discharge air temperature in the refrigerated case. They regulate the evaporator
pressure in order to provide the temperature required at
the discharge air sensor. EEPR valves can deliver a quick
temperature pulldown since they are sensing discharge
air instead of evaporator pressure. They will remain fully
open until the temperature of the discharge air in the case
drops to the valve’s set point. At this point, the valve will
start to throttle closed to maintain the desired discharge air
temperature.
A bipolar stepper motor operates the EEPR valve. The
stepper motor operates by moving the motor in discrete steps
instead of in a continuous rotation. The motor can step in
either direction because it has two different windings. A stepper motor may have as many as 100 electromagnets around
the rotor, resulting in an angular rotation that can be as small

Figure 25.11 EEPR valves in suction lines on a parallel compressor
system. Courtesy RSES Journal

Figure 25.10 An electric evaporator pressure-regulating (EEPR) valve.
Courtesy Sporlan Division, Parker Hannifin Corporation


---

## PDF Page 208

610

Section 5 Commercial Refrigeration

Figure 25.12 A stepper motor showing how the lead screw is driven
through gear reduction for providing additional power to the valve.

#### Stepper Motor

as 3.6° per step. Since a full circle is made up of 360°, an
angular rotation of 3.6° represents 1/100th of a rotation. The
motor drives a lead screw that converts the angular motion
into straight-line, or linear, motion, Figure 25.12, with a
resolution that can be as fine as 0.0000783 in. per step. An
algorithm programmed into a controller or microprocessor controls the direction and position of the stepper motor.
Refer to Unit 24, “Expansion Devices,” for a more detailed
explanation of bipolar stepper motor operation and control.

#### 25.3 Compressor Controls
#### And Oil-Related
#### COMPONENTS
#### Crankcase Pressure Regulator
The crankcase pressure-regulating (CPR) valve, Figure 25.13,
looks very much like the EPR valve, but it performs a different function. The CPR valve is used to limit the pressure
that returns to the compressor in the suction line. The CPR
valve, like the EPR valve, is installed in the suction line, but
it is usually located close to the compressor rather than at
the evaporator outlet. The CPR valve sensing bellows are
on the true compressor suction side of the valve and would

Figure 25.13 Three CPR valves that prevent the compressor from running in an overloaded condition during a hot pulldown. Courtesy Sporlan
Division, Parker Hannifin Corporation

normally have a gauge port on the evaporator side of the
valve, as shown in Figure 25.13.
A common application for the CPR valve is to keep a
low-temperature compressor from overloading during a hot
pulldown. A hot pulldown can occur:
-

-
-

when the compressor has been off for a long enough time
and the foodstuff has risen in temperature,
on start-ups with a warm box temperature, or
after a defrost period.

In any case, the temperature in the refrigerated box or the
evaporator influences the suction pressure. When the temperature is high, the suction pressure is high. When the suction
pressure goes up, the density of the suction gas goes up. The
compressor is a constant-volume pump and does not know
when the gas it is pumping is dense enough to create a motor
overload. When a compressor is started and the refrigerated
cooler is warmer than the design range of the compressor, an
overload can occur. The refrigeration compressor motor can
be operated at about 10% over its rated capacity without
harm during this pulldown. For example, when a motor has
a full-load amperage rating of 20 A, it could run at 22 A for
an extended pulldown and no motor harm would result. If
the temperature in the cooler were to be 75°F or 80°F, the
motor would be overloaded to the point that its overcurrent protection would shut it off. But this protection device
automatically resets, so the compressor would try to restart
immediately, causing it to short cycle. This type of operation
could continue until the manual reset motor control, the fuse,
or the breaker stopped the compressor, or until the system


---

## PDF Page 209

611

### Unit 25 Special Refrigeration System Components

compressor.

(CPR) valve. Courtesy Sporlan Valve Company

#### SUCTION
#### LINE

slowly pulls down during the brief running times. The CPR
valve throttles the suction gas entering the compressor to
keep the compressor current at no more than the rated value,
Figure 25.14.
The CPR valve is often referred to as a close on rise of
outlet pressure (CRO) valve. This is because it closes when its
outlet (crankcase) pressure rises to a predetermined pressure.
The CPR valve is controlled by crankcase (outlet) pressure
and its spring pressure. The crankcase pressure is a closing
force, and the spring pressure is an opening force. The inlet
pressure of the CPR valve is actually canceled out because
of the equal areas of the bellows and the seat disc. The inlet
pressure exerts an equal pressure on both the bellows and
seat disc, but these pressures are in opposite directions and
cancel one another out, Figure 25.15. The spring pressure can
be adjusted with a screwdriver or an Allen wrench to set the
maximum desired crankcase pressure.
During periods of high load, the CPR valve will be partially closed, holding some refrigerant back in the suction line.
When such is the case, there will be a pressure drop across
the CPR valve. When the load on the system is low and the

low-side pressure is below the set point on the CPR valve, the
valve will be fully open and the pressure at the valve’s outlet
will be equal to the pressure at its inlet. This, of course, will
only happen if the valve is properly adjusted.

#### Adjusting The Cpr Valve
The setting of the CPR valve should be accomplished on a hot
pulldown or at least when the compressor has enough load that
it is trying to run overloaded. This can be done by shutting off
the unit until the box or cooler is warm enough to create a load
on the evaporator. This loads up the compressor enough to cause
it to run at a high current. It is important to use an ammeter
while adjusting the CPR valve to ensure that the! amperage
draw of the compressor does not exceed its full-load amperage
rating. Adjusting the CPR valve is accomplished by changing
the spring pressure on the valve. Increasing the spring pressure
will increase the maximum suction pressure that can return to
the compressor, while decreasing the spring pressure decreases
the maximum suction pressure to the compressor.
For example, if a compressor is supposed to draw 20 amps
at fully loaded conditions and it is presently drawing 23 amps,
reduce the CPR valve’s spring pressure until the compressor
amperage drops to 20 A. Once this point is reached, the valve
is properly set. Under no circumstances will this compressor

during the off cycle. Courtesy AC & R Components, Inc.
#### Oil Return Line

#### COMPRESSOR

OIL
#### SEPARATOR

draw more than 20 amps. It is important to note that it is not
possible to properly adjust a CPR valve without running, and
monitoring the amperage draw of, the compressor.

#### Oil Separators
The oil separator, Figure 25.16, is installed in the discharge line
to separate the oil from the refrigerant and return the oil to the
compressor crankcase.
All reciprocating and rotary compressors allow a small amount of oil to pass through into the
discharge line. Once the oil leaves the compressor, it would
have to go through the entire system to get back to the compressor crankcase by way of the system piping and coils.
The oil separator has a reservoir and float assembly that
allows oil to return to the crankcase shortly after it leaves
the compressor.
Oil separators work on the principle of low refrigerant
and oil velocity. As oil-laden discharge gas enters the oil separator, its velocity is immediately slowed. This slowing of
velocity happens because of an abrupt change of direction,
impingement screens, internal baffling, or a large opening or
transitioning of the piping at the entrance of the oil separator.
Oil is usually mixed with the hot discharge gas in the form of
a fog. This refrigerant/oil fog runs into the internal baffling
or screens, which cause the refrigerant and oil fog to change
direction and rapidly slow down. Very fine particles of oil collide with one another and form larger, heavier particles. Fine
mesh impingement screens separate the oil and refrigerant
even more, causing large oil droplets to form and drop to the
bottom of the separator. The oil collects in a sump or reservoir
at the bottom of the separator. A magnet is often connected
to the bottom of the oil sump to collect any metallic particles.
When the oil level becomes high enough to raise a float,
an oil return needle valve is opened, and the oil is returned
to the compressor crankcase or oil reservoir through a small

#### CONDENSER

return line. This return line should be just above room temperature most of the time. If it is always hot, the float could
be stuck open, allowing hot gas to enter the crankcase, which
would also cause excessively high crankcase pressures and
could overheat the compressor, resulting in serious damage.
Because the oil separator is in the high side of the system
and the crankcase is in the low side, a pressure difference
naturally exists. This pressure difference drives the oil to
travel from the separator to the crankcase when the float is
in the right position. Only a small amount of oil is needed to
actuate the float mechanism. This ensures that only a small
amount of oil is ever absent from the compressor crankcase
at any given time.
Another type of oil separator is the helical oil separator,
Figure 25.17. Helical oil separators are about 98% efficient
in separating oil. When the oil and refrigerant fog enters the
oil separator, it hits the leading edge of a helical flighting. The
fog mixture is centrifugally forced along the spiral path of
the helix. This causes the heavier oil particles to be forced to
the perimeter of the separator’s shell. This is where impingement with a screen layer occurs. The screen layer acts as an
oil stripping and draining medium. The liquid oil then flows
through a baffle and into an oil collection area at the bottom
of the separator, and the oil-free refrigerant gas exits the separator just below the flighting. A float-activated oil return
valve allows the captured oil to return to the compressor’s
crankcase or oil reservoir.
The oil separator should be kept warm to keep liquid
refrigerant from condensing in it during the off cycle. The
float mechanism cannot distinguish between oil and liquid
refrigerant. If liquid refrigerant were in the separator, it
would return the refrigerant to the compressor crankcase,
which would dilute the oil and cause marginal lubrication.
If liquid refrigerant is returning to the compressor crankcase,
the return line between the separator and the crankcase will


---

#### Oil Return
#### LINE

#### COMPRESSOR

OIL
#### SEPARATOR

be cold from refrigerant vaporizing due to the pressure drop.
A damaged, leaking, or collapsed float in the oil separator
will cause the orifice to stay closed and excessive amounts of
oil will accumulate in the oil separator. This situation could
rob the system of its valuable lubricant and cause compressor
damage.

#### CONDENSER

#### Vibration Eliminators
Compressors produce enough vibration while running that
it is often necessary to protect the tubing at the suction and
discharge lines. Vibration can be eliminated on small compressors successfully with vibration loops. Large tubing
cannot be routed in loops, so special vibration eliminators,
Figure 25.18, are often installed. These are constructed with
Figure 25.18 Suction and discharge line vibration eliminators on a
semihermetic compressor. Courtesy Ferris State University. Photo by John
Tomczyk

#### Discharge Line
#### VIBRATION
#### ELIMINATOR

#### Suction Line
#### VIBRATION
#### ELIMINATOR

#### Bellowslike Interior

#### Wire Mesh
#### COVERING

a bellows-type lining and a flexible outer protective support,
Figure 25.19. These devices must be installed correctly, or
other problems, such as extra vibration, may occur. The
typical recommendation is that they be installed close to
the compressor and parallel to the compressor’s crankshaft,
Figure 25.20. If mounted crossways or perpendicular to the
crankshaft, excess vibration may occur. Follow the manufacturer’s directions.

#### Crankcase Heat
The crankcase heater, Figure 25.21, is designed to help protect the compressor from the effects of refrigerant migration.
Migration occurs when refrigerant liquid or vapor returns to
the compressor’s crankcase and mixes with the oil during the
off-cycle. If the compressor is started when liquid refrigerant
is present in the crankcase, compressor damage can result.
On start-up, the crankcase pressure reduces as soon as the
compressor starts to turn. The refrigerant will boil and the oil
will foam. The oil and refrigerant, some of which may be in
the liquid state, will be pumped out of the compressor. Valve
and bearing damage may occur and the compressor may be

operated with a limited oil charge until the oil returns to the
crankcase from the evaporator. The function of the crankcase heater, therefore, is to keep the oil in the compressor’s
crankcase at a temperature higher than the coldest part of the
system. This helps ensure that any liquid refrigerant entering
the crankcase will be vaporized and driven back into the suction line. It is recommended that crankcase heaters be energized for at least 24 hours before allowing the compressor to
operate.
The crankcase heater is a resistance heater that is strapped
on, inserted, or connected in some way to the compressor’s
crankcase. Crankcase heat can be applied in several ways.
Sometimes it is applied by heaters that are inserted into a well
located on the shell of the compressor’s crankcase where the
compressor’s oil can be heated, Figure 25.22, and sometimes
by external heaters at the base of the compressor adjacent to
the oil, Figure 25.23. When the heater is external, the heating element must be in good contact with the compressor
housing or it will overheat the element and not transfer heat
into the compressor. It is not uncommon to see the strap-on
type heater as shown in Figure 25.21. It is important that any
external crankcase heaters be installed correctly and tightly
to ensure that adequate heat is applied to the crankcase.
Since compressor oil is warm when the compressor is
operating, crankcase heaters are often wired in a manner that
Figure 25.22 Heater for inserting into a well inside the crankcase. Courtesy Ferris State University. Photo by John Tomczyk

Figure 25.21 Strap-on crankcase heater. Photo by Jason Obrzut

Figure 25.23 External loop heater for placement under a compressor’s
crankcase. Courtesy Ferris State University. Photo by John Tomczyk


---

## PDF Page 213

### Unit 25 Special Refrigeration System Components
Figure 25.24 A contactor with auxiliary contacts to control a crankcase
heater.
#### Normally Closed Contacts
#### Provide Crankcase Heat
#### Only During Off Cycle
L1

L2

L3

615

The technician should be well aware of crankcase heat.
If a compressor has been off for a long time, the housing
should be warm to the touch if it has crankcase heat. If the
manufacturer has installed crankcase heat, do not start the
compressor when it is cold, or damage may occur. In order to
avoid carbonization of the oil from excessive heat, the wattage inputs of the crankcase heater must be limited. In very
cold ambient or when exposed to cold winds, the crankcase
heater may have a limited effect and refrigerant migration to
the compressor’s crankcase may still occur.

#### Oil Pumps

#### Crankcase Heater In
#### Compressor Oil Sump

energizes them only when the compressor is not operating.
Manufacturers typically use relays to shut the crankcase heat
off during the running cycle, as heat added during the running
cycle reduces system efficiency. This can also be accomplished
with a set of normally closed auxiliary contactor contacts
that energize the crankcase heater when the compressor is
off. The heater contacts open when the main contactor contacts close to energize the compressor, Figure!25.24.
Crankcase heat is common in air-conditioning systems,
but is only beneficial when the heater is energized during the
off cycle. In some instances, the homeowner shuts off the disconnect at the outdoor condensing unit for the winter, leaving
the compressor without crankcase heat. If the homeowner
then starts the unit without allowing some time for the heat to
boil the refrigerant out of the oil, damage will likely occur to
the compressor. Compressors used on refrigeration typically
operate year-round and are not subject to seasonal shutdown.
However, if they are shut down for a period of time, the preceding situation will occur.
Crankcase heaters, as was seen, do help in combating
refrigerant migration. They do not, however, prevent slugging at start-ups or liquid floodback to compressors once the
compressor is running. Crankcase heaters, when used in conjunction with an automatic pumpdown cycle and/or suctionline accumulator, will help remedy many of these start-up
problems.

Smaller refrigeration compressors, typically under 3
horsepower, usually rely on a splash-type lubrication system
and do not require a physical oil pump. These smaller compressors often have an oil scoop that resembles a paddle that
flings oil throughout the compressor’s crankcase causing an
oil fog that lubricates the bearings, wrist pins, pistons, and
rings. However, larger compressors in the refrigeration and
air-conditioning fields have mechanical, forced lubrication
systems. These compressors are usually over 3 horsepower.
They contain an oil pump located at the end of the compressor’s crankshaft, Figure 25.25. The oil pump is actually
keyed into the compressor’s crankshaft which supplies power
to turn the oil pump, Figure 25.26. Oil pumps can be of the
gear or eccentric type. Figure 25.27 shows a gear type oil
pump. The star gear in this type of pump actually squeezes
the oil to a higher pressure. The added pressure that the star
gear puts into the oil can be referred to as the net oil pressure.
Figure 25.28 shows the star gear disassembled from the oil
pump and out of its seat within the oil pump.
One has to remember that the oil pump picks up the oil
from the compressor’s crankcase (under crankcase pressure),
adds pressure to the oil, and then delivers the oil into drilled
Figure 25.25 Oil pump located at end of the compressor and turned
by the compressor’s crankshaft. Courtesy Ferris State University. Photo by
John Tomczyk


---

## PDF Page 214

616

Section 5 Commercial Refrigeration

Figure 25.26 Oil pump is actually keyed into a slot into the compressor’s crankshaft. Courtesy Ferris State University. Photo by John Tomczyk

Figure 25.27 Gear type oil pump. Courtesy Ferris State University.
Photo by John Tomczyk

Figure 25.29 Eccentric type oil pump. Courtesy Ferris State University.
Photo by John Tomczyk

holes in the crankshaft. The oil is then delivered to the compressor’s bearings and connecting rods through these drilled
holes or oil galleys. The oil now drops to the crankcase to
be picked up again by the oil pump. Notice that the oil was
first picked up by the oil pump from the compressor’s crankcase, and after lubricating essential compressor parts, ends
up back in the crankcase. Figure 25.29 shows an eccentric
type oil pump which uses an eccentric type of mechanism to
squeeze the oil to a smaller and smaller volume, which adds
pressure to the oil. Again, this squeezing action is referred to
as net oil pressure.

#### Compressor Oil Check Valve
#### And Partition Wall

Figure 25.28 Oil pump with star gear disassembled. Courtesy Ferris
State University. Photo by John Tomczyk

Many compressors have a partition wall which separates the
crankcase from the motor barrel. On refrigerant gas-cooled
compressors, the motor barrel is where the motor windings
and stator reside and where oil entrained suction gases enter
the compressor’s end bell through the suction service valve.
Because the motor barrel has a large diameter compared to
the suction line, oil-entrained suction gases will lose velocity
and slow down significantly as they enter the motor barrel.
This loss of velocity will cause the oil in the suction gases
to drop out and collect at the bottom of the motor barrel.
The partition wall separating the motor barrel from the compressor’s crankcase has an oil check valve which will only let
oil pass from the compressor’s motor barrel to its crankcase,
Figure 25.30. When the compressor is running and no system
problems exist, there is usually a 1 to 2 psi pressure drop
between the motor barrel and the crankcase. This pressure
difference allows the oil check valve to open, which allows
any collected oil in the bottom of the motor barrel to return
to the compressor’s crankcase. The returned oil can now be
picked up by the oil pump and delivered throughout the compressor’s moving parts to provide lubrication.


---

## PDF Page 215

### Unit 25 Special Refrigeration System Components
Figure 25.30 Compressor’s crankcase showing partition wall, oil pickup
screen, and oil check valve. Courtesy Ferris State University. Photo by John
Tomczyk

617

method must be used to keep the head pressure up to an
acceptable level. The most common methods are as follows:
-
-
-
-

Fan cycling using a pressure control
Fan speed control
Air volume control using shutters and fan cycling
Condenser flooding and condenser splitting

This section provides an overview of commonly used
low-ambient controls. For more detailed coverage, please
refer to Section 22.18, “Head Pressure Controls” in Unit 22,
“Condensers.”

#### Fan-Cycling Head
#### Pressure Controls

However, during start-up periods or when some system
problems occur, the compressor’s crankcase could experience
a slightly higher pressure than the motor barrel. When this
occurs, the oil check valve will be closed, preventing any oil
return to the crankcase for use by the oil pump. An accumulation of oil will now occur in the motor barrel which
will prevent the compressor’s crankcase from receiving any
new oil from the motor barrel. If the system problem is not
remedied soon after start-up, the oil check valve will remain
closed and the crankcase will soon be starved of oil. This will
result in a loss of lubrication and a tripping of the oil safety
control. Some system problems that may cause this are:
-

-

-

-

-

Liquid refrigerant floodback during the on cycle from the
motor barrel to the crankcase.
Compressor start-ups with liquid refrigerant in the
crankcase.
Refrigerant migration to the crankcase from the suction
line and motor barrel during the off cycle causing start-up
pressurization of the crankcase. Refrigerant loves oil and
will migrate to it because of the oil’s lower vapor pressure.
No automatic pumpdown system to prevent refrigerant
migration.
Compressor located outside in a cold ambient, causing
refrigerant migration during the off cycle from suction line
through the motor barrel to the crankcase. This causes oil
foaming and crankcase pressurization at start-ups.

Fan cycling is a simple and reliable method to control and
maintain a refrigeration system’s head pressure. When there
are multiple condenser fans, some may be controlled by temperature, while the last fan can be controlled by head pressure. In this configuration, the control used is a pressure
control that closes on a rise in pressure to start the fan and
opens on a fall in pressure to stop the fan when the head
pressure falls, Figure 25.31. When fan cycling is used, the fan
will cycle on and off in the winter, but will run constantly in
hot weather. On multiple fan condensing units, one or more
ambient temperature controls may accomplish condenser fan
cycling. The controls are usually thermal disks located somewhere on the condensing unit’s frame, Figure 25.32. As the
outside ambient temperature drops, the fans begin to turn
off. As the outside ambient temperature rises, the fans begin
to turn on. Frequently, on smaller condensing units, one fan
will run continuously and the other fan will be cycled on and
off with an ambient temperature controller.
Using fan controls can be hard on a motor because of all
the cycling. The fan motor needs to be a type that does not
have a high starting current. In addition, the control must
Figure 25.31 A pressure control with the same action as a low-pressure
control, but at a higher pressure range than the low-pressure control.

#### 25.4 Low-Ambient Controls
Since refrigeration systems typically operate year-round,
low-ambient controls are an important part of a system’s
control strategy. When the condenser is located outside, the
head pressure will go down in the winter to the point that the
expansion valve will not have enough pressure drop across
it to feed refrigerant correctly. When this happens, some


---

## PDF Page 216

618

Section 5 Commercial Refrigeration

Figure 25.32 A thermal disk-type fan speed control. Courtesy Ferris
State University. Photo by John Tomczyk

condenser is installed either on the ground or on the roof, the
direction of airflow across the condenser’s face should be in
the prevailing wind direction. This way, when the condenser’s
fan is operating, it will not be bucking the prevailing winds
and decreasing airflow through the condenser.

#### Fan Speed Control For
#### Controlling Head Pressure

be rated to handle the current draw of the motor. To verify
this, the motor’s specifications should be carefully compared
to the control’s limitations and capabilities. Fan cycling can
vary the pressure to the expansion device a great deal. The
technician must choose whether to have the control set points
close together for the best expansion device performance or
far apart to keep the fan from short cycling. One application
for fan-cycling devices is the use of multiple fans, where one
fan operates continuously to minimize pressure fluctuation.
Vertical condensers may be affected by the prevailing
winds. If these winds are directed into the coil, they can
provide airflow similar to that of a fan. Sometimes a shield
is installed to prevent this, Figure 25.33. When an air-cooled
Figure 25.33 When fan-cycling controls are used, care must be taken
if the condenser is vertical. The prevailing winds can cause the head pressure to be too low even when the fan is off. A shield can be used to prevent the wind from affecting the condenser.

Devices that control fan speed have been used successfully in
many installations. These devices can be used with multiple
fans, where the first fans are cycled on and off by temperature and the speed of the last fan is controlled by head pressure or condensing temperature. The device that controls the
last fan is normally a transducer that converts a pressure or
temperature signal to an electrical signal that feeds a motor
speed controller. As the temperature drops on a cool day, the
motor speed is reduced. As the temperature rises, the motor
speed increases. At some predetermined point the additional
fans are started. On a hot day all fans will be running, with
the variable-speed fan running at maximum speed. Some
fan speed controls use a temperature sensor to monitor the
condenser’s temperature. Newer generation systems use variable frequency drives (VFDs) to control condenser fan motor
speed, Figure 25.34. A condenser fan motor is much more
energy efficient at reduced speeds. Fan motors with VFDs
have the ability to deliver variable air volume (VAV) flows.
This allows the condenser fan to provide the correct amount
of airflow through the condenser coil that is needed under
a certain ambient condition. VFDs can control the speed of
a condenser fan motor by controlling the frequency of the
power signal supplied to the motor. The lower the frequency,
the slower the motor, and vice versa. For a more detailed
explanation of VFD-controlled motors, refer to Section
17.28, “Inverters and Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)” in
Unit 17, “Types of Electric Motors.”

Figure 25.34 Four VFDs used to control the speed of a motor by varying the frequency delivered to the motor. Courtesy Ferris State University.
Photo by John Tomczyk

#### Air Volume Control For
#### Controlling Head Pressure
Air volume control using shutters can be accomplished with
a piston-driven damper controlled by the high-pressure
refrigerant, Figure 25.35. As the system’s high-side pressure
rises, the dampers are pushed open to allow more air to pass
through the condenser coil. As the system’s high-side pressure
drops, the dampers are pushed closed, reducing the amount
of air passing through the condenser coil.
When there are multiple fans, the first fans can be cycled
off using temperature, and the shutter can be used on the
last fan, Figure 26.36. This system results in a steady head
pressure, as with fan speed control. The expansion valve inlet

pressure does not fluctuate as it does when one fan is cycled
on and off. The shutter can be located either on the fan inlet
or on the fan outlet. Be careful that the fan motor is not overloaded if the damper is positioned on the discharge of the fan.

#### Condenser Flooding And
#### Condenser Splitting For
#### Controlling Head Pressure
Condenser flooding devices, Figure 25.37, are used in both
mild and cold weather to cause the refrigerant to move from
an oversized receiver into the condenser. This three-port valve
controls the flow of liquid refrigerant from the condenser while

operates the shutters. This application modulates the airflow and gives steady head pressure control.

## PDF Page 218

620

Section 5 Commercial Refrigeration

Figure 25.37 A condenser flooding valve. Courtesy Sporlan Valve
Company

regulating the flow of hot compressor discharge gas, around
the condenser, directly to the receiver, Figure 25.38. The sensed
pressure at the outlet, receiver side, of the valve controls the
position of the valve. As the high-side pressure increases, during periods of warm ambient temperature, the valve throttles
open to allow more refrigerant to flow from the condenser
outlet to the receiver and reduces the flow of discharge gas
from the compressor directly to the receiver. When the ambient temperature is very high, no hot discharge gas bypasses
the condenser. The OROA notation (open on rise of outlet) on
the valve indicates that it opens the connection between the
condenser and the receiver on a rise of outlet pressure.
When the ambient temperature is low, the high-side pressure drops and the valve port connected to the outlet of the
condenser starts to throttle closed. At the same time, the connection between the hot discharge gas port and the receiver
throttles open, allowing hot gas to bypass the condenser coil.
This causes the high-side pressure to rise. During periods of
low-ambient temperature, refrigerant backs up into the condenser. This excess refrigerant in the condenser acts like an
overcharge and causes the head pressure to be much higher

Figure 25.38 The gas going to the receiver keeps the pressure up
for correct expansion valve operation. The gas going to the condenser
is changed to a liquid and subcooled in the condenser. It then returns
to the receiver to subcool the remaining liquid. Courtesy Sporlan Valve
Company

than it normally would be on a mild or cold day. On warmer
days, the excess refrigerant is directed back to the receiver.
This method of head pressure control gives very steady pressure control while the system is running.
The system requires a large amount of refrigerant because,
in addition to the normal operating charge, there must be
enough refrigerant to flood the condenser in the winter. A large
receiver is used to store the refrigerant in the summer, when
valves will divert the excess liquid into the receiver. Condenser
flooding has one added benefit in the winter. Because the condenser is nearly full of refrigerant, the liquid refrigerant that is
furnished to the expansion devices is well below the condensing temperature. Remember, subcooling will help improve the
efficiency of the system. The liquid may be subcooled to well
below freezing for systems operating in a cold climate.
One way to reduce the amount of extra refrigerant charge
needed for condenser flooding is to split the condenser into
two separate and identical condenser circuits, Figure 25.39.
This is referred to as condenser splitting. The splitting of the
condensers is done with the addition of a pilot-operated,
three-way solenoid valve installed in the discharge line from
the compressor. The splitting of the identical condensers is
done in such a way that only one half of the condenser is used
for winter operation, and both halves are used for summer
operation. The top half of the condenser is referred to as the
“Summer/Winter” condenser, and the bottom half of the condenser is referred to as the “Summer” condenser. The threeway solenoid valve controlling the splitting of the condensers
can be energized and deenergized by a controller sensing outside ambient, an outdoor thermostat, or a high-side pressure
control. For a more detailed explanation of head pressure
controls, refer to Section 22.18, “Head Pressure Controls” in
Unit 22, “Condensers.”

Figure 25.39 Split condensers with a three-way, solenoid-operated
split condenser valve incorporated into a refrigeration system. Courtesy
Sporlan Division, Parker Hannifin Corporation

## PDF Page 219

### Unit 25 Special Refrigeration System Components

#### 25.5 Pressure Switches And
#### CONTROLS
Pressure switches are used to stop and start electrical current
flow to refrigeration components. They play a very important part in the function of the equipment. The typical pressure switch can be
-
-
-
-

a low-pressure switch—closes on a rise in pressure,
a high-pressure switch—opens on a rise in pressure,
a low-ambient control—closes on a rise in pressure, or
an oil safety switch—has a time delay; opens on a rise in
pressure.

#### Low-Pressure Switch
The low-pressure switch or control, Figure 25.40, is used in
the following two major refrigeration applications:
-
-

Low-charge protection
Control of space temperature

The low-pressure control can be used as a low-charge protection by setting the control to cut out at a value that is below
the typical evaporator operating pressure. For example, in
a medium temperature cooler the air temperature may be
expected to be no lower than 34°F. When this cooler uses
R-134a for the refrigerant, the lowest pressure at which the
evaporator would be expected to operate would be around
18 psig because the coil would normally operate at about
15°F colder than the air temperature in the coil (34°F 2 15°F
5 19°F). The refrigerant in the coil should not operate below

Figure 25.40 The low-pressure control. The control contacts close on a
rise in pressure and open on a fall in pressure. Photo by Bill Johnson

621

this temperature of 19°F, which converts to 18 psig. The
low-pressure cut-out should be set below the expected operating condition of 18 psig and above the atmospheric pressure of 0 psig. A setting that would cut off the compressor at
5 psig would keep the low-pressure side of the system from
going into a vacuum in the event of refrigerant loss. This setting would be well below the typical operating condition.
The control would normally be automatically reset. When a
low-charge condition exists, the control would cut the compressor off and on and maintain some refrigeration. A store
owner may call with a complaint that the unit is cutting off
and on but not lowering the cooler temperature properly.
The cut-in setting for this application should be a pressure that is below the pressure corresponding to the highest
temperature the cooler is expected to experience in a typical
cycle and just lower than the pressure corresponding to the
thermostat cut-in point. For example, the thermostat may be
set at 45°F, which would mean that the pressure in the evaporator may rise as high as 40 psig. The low-pressure control should be set to cut in at about 25 psig. The previous
example of a low-pressure control set to cut out at 5 psig
and to cut in at 40 psig means that the control differential is
35 psig. Remember that the compressor is not running. The
wide differential helps to prevent the compressor from coming back on too soon or from short cycling.

#### Low-Pressure Control
#### Applied As A Thermostat
The low-pressure control setup described in the previous
example is for low-charge protection only, to keep the system
from going into a vacuum. The same control can be set to
operate the compressor to maintain the space temperature
in the cooler and to serve as a low-charge protection as well.
Using the same temperatures as in the preceding example
as the operating conditions, 34°F and 45°F, the low-pressure
control can be set to cut out when the low pressure reaches
18 psig. This corresponds to a coil temperature of 19°F. When
the air in the cooler reaches 34°F, the coil temperature should
be 19°F, with a corresponding pressure of 18 psig. This system has a room-to-coil temperature difference of 15°F (34°F
room temperature 2 19°F coil temperature 5 15°F temperature difference). When the compressor cuts off, the air in the
cooler is going to raise the coil temperature to 34°F and a
corresponding pressure of 30 psig. As the cooler temperature goes up, it will raise the temperature of the refrigerant.
When the temperature of the air increases to 45°F, the coil
temperature should be 45°F with a corresponding pressure
of 40 psig. This could be the cut-in point of the low-pressure
control. The settings would be cut out at 18 psig and cut in at
40 psig. This is a differential of 22 psig and would maintain
a cooler temperature of 34°F to 45°F. You could say that you
are using the refrigerant in the evaporator coil as the thermostat sensing fluid.


---

## PDF Page 220

622

Section 5 Commercial Refrigeration

Figure 25.41 This table is to be used as a guide for setting low-pressure controls for the different applications. Courtesy Tecumseh Products Company

One of the advantages of this type of control arrangement
is that no interconnecting wires must be installed between the
inside of the cooler and the condensing unit. If a thermostat
is used to control the air temperature in the cooler, a pair of
wires must be run between the condensing unit and the inside
of the cooler. In some installations, the condensing unit is a
considerable distance from the cooler, which makes running
wires impractical. With the temperature being controlled at
the condensing unit, an owner is less likely to adjust the control and potentially cause problems.
As many low-pressure control settings exist as there are
applications. Different situations call for different settings.
Figure 25.41 is a chart of settings recommended by one
company.
Low-pressure controls are rated by their pressure range
and the current draw of the contacts. A low-pressure control
that is suitable for R-134a may not be suitable for use with
R-404A because of their different pressure ranges. For the
same application in the previous paragraph using R-404A,
the cut-out would be 55 psig and the cut-in would be 95 psig.
The correct control for the pressure range must be chosen.
Some controls are single-pole–double-throw, which can make
or break on a rise. Such controls can serve as one component serving two different functions. The contact rating for a
pressure control has to do with the size of electrical load the
control can carry. If the pressure control is expected to start
a small compressor, the inrush current should also be considered. Normally a pressure control used for refrigeration
is rated so that it can directly start up to a 3-hp single-phase
compressor. If the compressor is any larger, or three phase,
a contactor or motor starter is normally used. The pressure
control then can control the contactor or motor starter coil,
Figure 25.42.

Figure 25.42 When a compressor that is larger than the electrical rating of the pressure control contacts is encountered, the compressor has
to be started with a contactor. The wiring diagram shows how this is
accomplished.

#### High-Pressure Control
The high-pressure control, Figure 25.43, is usually not as
complicated as the low-pressure control. It is used to keep
the compressor from operating with a high head pressure. This control is necessary on water-cooled equipment
because an interruption of water is more likely than an
interruption of air. This control opens on a rise in pressure and should be set above the typical high pressure that
the machine would normally encounter. The high-pressure
control may be either automatic or manual reset. With aircooled condensers, the condenser saturation temperature
should be no more than 30°F higher than the temperature


---

## PDF Page 221

### Unit 25 Special Refrigeration System Components
Figure 25.43 A high-pressure switch to keep the compressor from running when high system pressures occur.

623

Some high-pressure controls have a fixed differential of
50 psig. This means they will cycle the compressor back on
at 50 psig lower than the cut-out setting. For example, if a
system cycles off on the high-pressure control at 250 psig, it
will come back on at 200 psig (250 psig – 50 psig).
Some manufacturers specify a manual reset high-pressure
control. When this control cuts out, someone must press the
reset button to start the compressor. This calls attention to the
fact that a problem exists. The manual reset control provides
better equipment protection, but the automatic reset control
may save the food by allowing the compressor to run at short
intervals. An observant owner or operator should notice the
short cycle of the automatic control if the compressor is near
the workspace.

#### Low-Ambient Fan Control

of the ambient air. If the ambient air is 95°F, the condenser
would be operating at about 185 psig if the system used
R-134a (95°F 1 30°F 5 125°F condensing temperature,
which corresponds to 185 psig). The high-pressure control
should be set well above 185 psig for the R-134a system.
If the control were set to cut out at 250 psig, there should
be no interference with normal operation, and it still would
give good protection. Most condensers operate at a 30°F
temperature difference as shipped from the factory and will
continue to operate under this condition when properly
maintained. Many condensers for low-temperature applications may operate at temperature differences as low as 15°F
to maintain low compression ratios.
The control cut-in point must be above the pressure
corresponding to the ambient temperature of 95°F. If the
compressor cuts off and the outdoor fan continues to run,
the temperature inside the condenser will quickly reach the
ambient temperature. For example, if the ambient is 95°F,
the pressure will quickly fall to 114 psig for an R-134a
system. If the high-pressure control were set to cut in at
135!psig, the compressor could come back on with a safe
differential of 115 psig (cut-out 250 – cut-in 135 5 115
psig differential).

The low-ambient fan control, Figure 25.44, has the same
switch action as the low-pressure control but operates at a
higher pressure range. This control stops and starts the condenser fan in response to head pressure. This control must
be coordinated with the high-pressure control to keep them
from working against each other. The high-pressure control
stops the compressor when the head pressure gets too high,
and the low-ambient control starts the fan when the pressure
gets to a predetermined point before the high-pressure control stops the compressor.
When a low-ambient control is used, the high-pressure
cut-out should be set to a pressure that is higher than the cutin point of the low-ambient control. For example, if the lowambient control is set to maintain the head pressure between
125 and 175 psig, a high-pressure control setting of 250 psig
should not interfere with the low-ambient control setting.
Figure 25.44 The low-ambient control used to open the contacts on a
drop in head pressure to stop the condenser fan.


---

## PDF Page 222

624

Section 5 Commercial Refrigeration

This can easily be verified by installing a gauge and stopping
the condenser fan to make sure that the high-pressure control
is cutting out where it is supposed to.
With the gauge on the high side, the fan action can also be
observed to see that the fan is cycling off and on as it should.
The fan will operate all of the time on high-ambient days,
and the fan control will not stop the fan. The low-ambient
pressure control can be identified by the terminology on the
control’s action. It is described as “close on a rise” in pressure. This is the same terminology used on a low-pressure
control. The difference is that the low-ambient fan control
has a higher operating pressure range.

#### Oil Pressure Safety Control
Many larger refrigeration and air-conditioning compressors
have forced oiling systems. These compressors are usually
over 3 hp. They contain an oil pump located at the end of the
compressor crankshaft, Figure 25.27. The crankshaft is connected to the oil pump and supplies power, which turns the
oil pump, which can be either of the gear or eccentric type.
Figure 25.27 shows a gear type oil pump and Figure 25.29
illustrates an eccentric type oil pump. The oil pump forces oil
through holes drilled in the crankshaft and delivers it to bearings and connecting rods. The oil then drops to the crankcase
to be picked up again by the oil pump. Smaller compressors
usually have a splash-type oiling system with a scoop that
scoops and flings the oil throughout the crankcase, causing
an oil fog as the crankshaft turns.
The oil pressure safety switch, Figure 25.45, is used to ensure
that the compressor has oil pressure when operating.
The oil pressure control is used on larger compressors
and has a different sensing arrangement than do the highand low-pressure controls. The high- and low-pressure

Figure 25.45 Oil safety controls have a bellows on each side. They
are opposed in their forces and measure the net oil pressure. Photo by
Bill Johnson

controls are single-diaphragm or single-bellows controls
because they are comparing atmospheric pressure with the
pressures inside the system. Atmospheric pressure can be considered a constant for any particular locality because it does
not vary more than a small amount. The oil pressure safety
control, on the other hand, is a pressure differential control.
It measures the difference between two pressures to determine if a net positive oil pressure is present. One pressure is
the compressor crankcase pressure, which is the same as the
compressor suction pressure. The suction pressure will vary
from the off or standing reading to the actual running reading, not to mention the reading that would occur when a low
charge is experienced. For example, when a system is using
R-22 as the refrigerant, the pressures may be similar to the
following: 125 psig while standing, 70 psig while operating,
and 20 psig during a low-charge situation. The other pressure
is the oil pump’s outlet pressure. The difference between these
two pressure is called the net oil pressure.
When dealing with compressors that employ an oil
pump, many service technicians confuse net oil pressure with
oil pump discharge pressure. However, it is of utmost importance that technicians understand the difference between these
two pressures when servicing compressors with oil pumps.
The oil pump’s rotating gear or eccentrics add a certain pressure to the oil pumped through the crankshaft. This pressure
is considered net oil pressure.
NET OIL PRESSURE CALCULATION. Net oil pressure is not
the pressure that can be measured at the discharge or outlet of the oil pump. The oil pump picks up oil at suction or
crankcase pressure from the compressor’s crankcase through
a screen or filter. The oil pump discharge port’s pressure
includes both crankcase pressure and the oil pump gear pressure it adds to the oil. This is why net oil pressure cannot be
measured directly with a gauge. A gauge at the oil pump’s
discharge port would register a combination of crankcase
pressure and oil pump gear pressure. The technician must
subtract the crankcase pressure from the discharge port pressure to get the net oil pressure. The following is an equation
for net oil pressure:

Oil Pump Discharge Pressure 5
Crankcase Pressure 1 Net Oil Pressure
Example: The oil pump discharge pressure is 80 psig. The
crankcase pressure is 20 psig. What would be the net oil pressure?
Solution: Simply subtract the crankcase pressure from the oil
pump discharge pressure to get net oil pressure.
80 psi 2 20 psi 5 60 psid net oil pressure
This means the oil pump is actually putting 60 psid of
pressure into the oil when delivering it into the crankshaft’s
drilled passages. Notice that the net oil pressure is expressed
in pounds per square inch differential (psid) because it is a
difference between two pressures.


---

## PDF Page 223

625

### Unit 25 Special Refrigeration System Components

Sometimes the compressor’s crankcase may be operating
in a vacuum. In this case, the crankcase pressure is negative.
Remember that every 2 in. of mercury vacuum is equivalent
to approximately 1 psi.
Example: What is the net oil pressure if the oil pump discharge pressure is 35 psig and the crankcase pressure is 6 in.
of vacuum (23 psi)?
Solution: Again, we must subtract the crankcase pressure
from the oil pump discharge pressure to get the net oil
pressure.
35 psi 2 (23 psi) 5 38 psid net oil pressure
This means that the oil pump is delivering 38 psid of
net oil pressure through the crankshaft and bearings. The
terms suction pressure and crankcase pressure are often used
interchangeably in the HVACR industry. However, on some
larger refrigerant-cooled compressors there is a partition wall
between the crankcase and motor barrel, Figure 25.30. During start-up periods or when system problems occur, there
could be a slight difference in pressure between crankcase
pressure in the crankcase and suction pressure in the motor
barrel. Because of this, it is important to use the crankcase
pressure, not the suction pressure, when connecting an oil
safety controller to these types of compressors.
In the basic low-pressure control, the pressure is under
the diaphragm or bellows and the atmospheric pressure is
on the other side. The oil pressure control uses a double
bellows—one bellows opposing the other—to detect the net
or actual oil pressure, Figure 25.46. The pump inlet pressure
is under one bellows, and the pump outlet pressure is under
the other bellows. These bellows are opposite each other
either physically or by linkage. The bellows with the most
pressure is the oil pump outlet, and it overrides the bellows
with the least amount of pressure. This override reads out
in net pressure and is attached to a linkage that can stop
the compressor when the net pressure drops for a predetermined time.
Figure 25.46 Internal view of an oil pressure safety control. Photo by
Bill Johnson

Because the control
needs a pressure differential to allow power to pass to the
compressor, it must have some means of allowing the compressor to initially start. There is no pressure differential until
the compressor starts to turn because the oil pump is attached
to the compressor crankshaft. Therefore, a time delay is built
into the control to allow the compressor to get started and
to prevent nuisance cut-outs when oil pressure may vary for
only a moment. This time delay is normally about 90 to 120
sec. It is accomplished with a heater circuit and a bimetal
device or electronic circuitry, Figure 25.47. The delay also
gives the crankcase time to clear any unwanted refrigerant
during periods when refrigerant migration or flooding has
occurred. Furthermore, it avoids shutdowns during short
fluctuations in net oil pressure on start-up.
Net oil pressures vary from compressor to compressor.
They usually range from 20 to 40 psi. Most oil pressure
safety controllers will shut the compressor down if the net oil
pressure falls below 10 psi. The following variables affect the
net oil pressure:

#### Oil Pressure Switch Time Delay.

-
-
-
-
-

Compressor size
Oil viscosity
Oil temperature
Bearing clearance
Percent of refrigerant in the oil

Larger compressors need more net oil pressure because they
have more surface areas to lubricate, and the oil pumps must
pump and carry the oil greater distances. As the oil gets hotter
and its viscosity drops, the net oil pressure will also usually
drop. As a compressor wears, its tolerances become greater and
it becomes easier for the oil to escape through these clearances.
Figure 25.47 The oil pressure control is used for lubrication protection
for the compressor. The oil pump that lubricates the compressor is driven
by the compressor crankshaft.

TRANSDUCER-TYPE OIL SAFETY CONTROLLERS. Transducer-

type oil safety controllers use a pressure transducer, which
senses a combination of oil pump discharge pressure and
crankcase pressure. The pressure transducer has two separate
ports to sense both crankcase pressure and oil pump discharge
pressure. The difference between these two pressures, the net
oil pressure, is mechanically determined by the pressure transducer. The transducer is connected to an electronic controller by wires, Figure 25.48. The pressure transducer is shown
connected to the compressor in Figure 25.49. It then transforms a pressure signal to an electrical signal for the electronic

a three-phase motor with internal overload.

controller to process. The advantage of an electronic oil safety
controller is that it eliminates the need for the transmission
lines that are found on traditional oil pressure safety switches.
This reduces the chance of refrigerant leaks. Also, the electronic clock and circuitry are much more accurate and reliable.
Remember, when the compressor is off, the net oil pressure is
0 psi and the differential pressure switch contacts are closed.
The heater in the oil safety controller will not be energized
during the off cycle because it is wired to the load side of the
motor starter contacts, Figure 25.50. When the motor starter
contacts are opened, L2 is taken out of the heater circuit. At
start-up, when the motor starter contacts close and the compressor starts, the differential pressure switch contacts will stay
closed and the heater will be energized until at least 9 psid of
net oil pressure is developed on most controllers. As discussed
previously, this time delay will prevent nuisance trips to the
controller at compressor start-ups or when system problems
like floodback and migration occur.
If a motor is equipped with both an
internal inherent motor protector and an oil safety controller, the oil safety controller may trip on some systems due

#### Internal Overloads


---

## PDF Page 225

627

### Unit 25 Special Refrigeration System Components

to a motor overheating or overloading problem. When the
internal overload opens, the motor is shut off but the motor
starter coil remains energized with contacts closed. The
2-min timing circuit would be activated due to the lack of net
oil pressure. This will trip the oil safety controller in a matter
of 2 min because the heater will still be energized through
the load side of the motor starter, Figure 25.50. Remember,
there is no net oil pressure when the compressor is shut off
by its internal overload protector. In time, the compressor
will cool off and the internal overload will reset (close). But
the compressor will not restart until pushing the reset button
manually resets the oil safety controller. This condition can
be prevented by the use of a current sensing relay.
The current sensing relay, Figure 25.51, allows the compressor to cycle on the internal overload while not affecting
the operation of the oil safety controller. The current sensing relay is wired to the load side of the contactor or motor
starter. It acts like an inductive-type ammeter. One leg of the
load side of the compressor motor is passed through the relay
inductive coil. The inductive coil controls a normally open set
of contacts in series with the time delay heater, Figure!25.52.

Figure 25.51 A current sensing relay. Photo by John Tomczyk

One leg of the compressor motor is shown passing through
the inductive coil in Figure 25.51. The contacts are closed
when normal motor current is sensed. When the current drops
quite low, or reduces to zero because of the motor being off,
the contacts of the current relay will open and take power
away from the time delay heater. This prevents a tripping of
the oil safety controller in case the internal overload opens
(compressor off) but the motor starter’s coil is still energized.
The voltage dropping resistor in Figure 25.52 makes the control more versatile for use on 230-V or 115-V systems.
In these situations, it is often necessary to incorporate
another field-wired relay when dealing with an electronic oil
safety controller. The reason for this is that the electronic oil
safety module requires power to the module for proper reset.
The added relay simply provides this power to the module
during an oil safety trip. However, always consult with the
compressor and control manufacturer before applying or
wiring any of these devices or when working with any compressor that has an oil safety control.

#### 25.6 Solenoid Valves
#### And The Automatic
#### Pumpdown Cycle
#### The Solenoid Valve
The solenoid valve, Figure 25. 53, is the component most
frequently used to control fluid flow. The valve has a magnetic coil that, when energized, will lift a plunger into the
coil, Figure 25.54. This valve can be either normally open
(NO) or normally closed (NC). The NC valve is closed until
energized; then it opens. The NO valve is normally open until
Figure 25.53 Electrically operated solenoid valves. The valve is moved
by the plunger attached to the seat. The plunger moves into the magnetic coil when the coil is energized to either open or close the valve.
Courtesy Sporlan Division, Parker Hannifin Corporation

Figure 25.54 Cutaway view of an electrically operated solenoid valve.
Courtesy Sporlan Division, Parker Hannifin Corporation

#### PLUNGER

#### Magnetic Wiring
#### COIL
#### HOUSING

INL

ET

OU
TLE

T

#### Manual Open Stem

energized; then it closes. The plunger is attached to the valve
so that the plunger action moves the valve. Solenoid valves
must be sized correctly for their particular application. For
the refrigerant liquid line, they are sized according to refrigerant tonnage and a pressure drop through the valve that is
acceptable. If a valve needs to be replaced, the manufacturer’s
data will help you choose the correct valve.
Solenoid valves can be used to control either liquid or
vapor flow. They are snap-acting valves and open and close
very fast when electrical energy is applied to the coil. The
snap action can cause liquid hammer when installed in a
liquid line, however, so care must be taken when locating the
valve. Liquid hammer occurs when the fast-moving liquid
is shut off by the solenoid valve, causing the liquid to stop
abruptly. The solenoid valve must be fastened securely to the
refrigerant line by flare, flange, or solder connections. Most
valves have to be serviced at some time. The valves that are
soldered in the line can be serviced easily if they can be disassembled. The manufacturer’s instructions as to the location and placement of solenoid valves should be followed. In
order to operate properly, the solenoid valve must be oriented
in the correct direction to the fluid flow or the valve may not
close tightly, Figure 25.55. The valve has an arrow that must
point in the direction of flow. When positioned correctly, the
pressure of the fluid in the valve helps to close the valve. If the
high pressure is under the valve seat, the plunger may have
a tendency to lift the valve off the seat. Most solenoid valves
have a heavy plunger that is lifted to open the valve. When
the plunger is not energized, its weight holds the valve on
its sealing seat. If this type of valve is installed on its side or
upside down, the valve will remain in the energized position
when it should be closed, Figure 25.56.


---

629

### Unit 25 Special Refrigeration System Components
Figure 25.55 The fluid flow in a solenoid valve must be in the correct
direction, or the valve will not close tight. If the high-pressure fluid is
under the seat, it will have a tendency to raise the valve off the seat. When
installed correctly, the fluid helps to hold the valve on the seat.

Figure 25.57 A pilot-operated solenoid valve. The magnetic coil controls a small line that directs the high-pressure refrigerant to one end of a
sliding piston. The magnetic coil has to do only a small amount of work,
and the difference in pressure does the rest. Based on Alco Controls Division, Emerson Electric Company.

#### FLOW

A special solenoid valve known as pilot-operated valve
is available for controlling fluid flow in larger applications.
This valve uses a very small valve seat to divert high-pressure
gas that causes a larger valve to change position, thus using
the difference in pressure to cause a large movement. In
pilot-operated valves, the solenoid’s magnetic coil only has to
lift a small seat. An electrical coil large enough to cause the
switching action of a large valve would be very big and draw
too much current for practical use, Figure 25.57. Pilot action

reduces the size of the electrical coil and the overall size of the
valve. Pilot-acting valves are used when large vapor or liquid
lines must be switched and can have more than one inlet and
one outlet. Some are known as four-way valves; others as
three-way valves.

#### Automatic Pumpdown Systems
The automatic pumpdown system consists of a normally
closed liquid-line solenoid valve installed in the liquid line of a
refrigeration system. The normally closed solenoid valve is controlled by a thermostat that is located somewhere in the refrigerated space. When the desired box temperature is reached
in the refrigerated space, the thermostat will open and deenergize the liquid-line solenoid valve, causing the valve to close.


---

## PDF Page 228

630

Section 5 Commercial Refrigeration

This will stop the flow of refrigerant into the metering device
and evaporator. The compressor will continue to run, pumping
any refrigerant downstream of the closed solenoid valve to the
high side, causing the low-side pressure to drop. Figure 25.58
is an electrical schematic diagram of an automatic pumpdown
system using a liquid-line solenoid valve, thermostat, and
low-pressure control. Figure!25.59 is a system diagram showing the electrical hookup of an automatic pumpdown system.
Refrigerant pumped by the compressor from the low side
of the system will be stored in the condenser and receiver on
the high side of the refrigeration system. The compressor will
shut off by the action of a low-pressure control set to open at
about 5 to 10 psig, which ensures that no refrigerant migrates
to the compressor during the off cycle. On a call for cooling,
the thermostat closes and energizes the liquid-line solenoid
valve. This action sends liquid refrigerant to the expansion valve and into the empty evaporator, thus increasing
